content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Five months ago, I thought I knew what AI agents needed.
Like many developers, I believed the biggest limitation was capability.
If an agent could learn more skills…
If it could write its own skills…
If it could accumulate experience over time…
Surely it would become better.
That’s exactly why I started experimenting with Hermes Agent.
I had heard people say it was more autonomous than many other open-source agents. It could even generate its own skills over time.
That sounded like the future.
So I forked it, deployed it, and started using it every day.
The deeper I went, the more I became fascinated by agent skills.

My first conclusion: Skill management is the biggest problem
As I used Hermes more, I noticed something interesting.
The number of skills kept growing.
Some skills overlapped.
Some were outdated.
Some were never used again.
And there was no easy way to understand whether a skill was actually helping the agent or simply adding more complexity.
It reminded me of a problem every software project eventually faces.
More code doesn’t automatically create a better system.
More features don’t necessarily create a better product.
Maybe…
More skills don’t necessarily create a better agent.
That realization led me to build Skill Inspector.
The idea was simple:
Instead of letting agents accumulate skills forever, we should inspect them.
Measure them.
Detect duplicates.
Find obsolete skills.
Remove low-value ones.
At the time, I genuinely believed this was one of the biggest problems in autonomous agents.

Then I started looking outside my own project
While working on Skill Inspector, I spent a lot of time reading discussions from developers building AI agents.
I watched conversations around Claude Code.
I followed people using OpenClaw.
I read feedback from Hermes users.
I also looked back at my own automation experiments.
Something started to stand out.
Everyone was running into surprisingly similar problems.
Not “my agent needs another skill.”
Instead:

• Agents silently retrying the same failing action.

• Tool calls reporting success when the real task had failed.

• Infinite loops caused by tiny execution errors.

• Long-running tasks gradually drifting away from their original goal.

• No clear way to understand why an agent eventually failed.
Different frameworks.
Different models.
Almost the same failures.
That changed how I looked at the problem.

The moment I changed my mind
One of my own automation tasks was calling an API through a shell command.
A tiny shell syntax issue caused the command to fail.
The interesting part wasn’t the bug itself.
It was what happened next.
The agent kept trying.
Again.
And again.
Nothing meaningful was happening anymore.
It wasn’t reasoning.
It wasn’t planning.
It wasn’t intelligence.
It was execution.
That made me realize something uncomfortable.
The agent wasn’t failing because it couldn’t think.
It was failing because it couldn’t reliably execute.

I don’t think reliability is a Hermes problem
The more I observed different agent frameworks, the more I noticed they were all struggling with similar issues.
Whether it’s Hermes.
OpenClaw.
Claude Code.
Or other agent frameworks.
Everyone is trying to build smarter agents.
But relatively few people are focused on making agents reliable over long-running tasks.
That’s where I think one of the biggest opportunities is.

I’m changing the direction of my project
Instead of continuing to build more standalone skills, I’m now focusing on something much bigger:
Agent Reliability.
Not by rewriting the agent itself.
But by improving how it executes work.
The first milestone is surprisingly small:
Tool Execution Journal.
Every tool execution should become observable.
Every failure should be traceable.
Every retry should be explainable.
Because before agents can recover from failures…
We first need to understand how they fail.
After that comes:

• Tool verification

• Checkpoint & resume

• Recovery policies
Step by step.

I’m still figuring it out
To be honest, this isn’t a success story.
I’ve spent about five months building in this space.
I have users.
I’ve learned a lot.
But I still haven’t earned my first dollar.
There were moments when I seriously considered giving up.
There were moments when I questioned whether I was solving the right problem at all.
Ironically, that’s exactly how I ended up here.
Not by stubbornly sticking to my original idea.
But by letting real-world failures change my mind.
Maybe I’ll discover six months from now that I’m wrong again.
If that happens, I’ll write about it too.
For now, though, I think reliable AI agents matter more than agents with one more skill.
And that’s what I’m building next.
